subtopic_detail: Choice of first-line trauma focused therapy in adult PTSD with single index event
stem: You are the community psychiatry registrar reviewing a 34-year-old woman with 4 months of intrusive memories, avoidance, hyperarousal, and nightmares after a single motor vehicle crash. She is mid-grade depressed but not suicidal. She is reluctant to take medication and is asking about psychological treatment options. She has access to both a clinical psychologist with EMDR training and a clinical psychologist with TF-CBT training within the same service.

data_table.Examination: Mental state alert, mood low-mid, no psychosis, no suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: Nil indicated; PCL-5 score 52, PHQ-9 score 13

lead_in: Which statement is the most accurate first-line recommendation under AU guidance?

options.text: Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing has clearly superior outcomes to trauma focused CBT in adult PTSD
options.rationale: Head-to-head trials show comparable outcomes between TF-CBT and EMDR; neither is clearly superior. (Source: Phoenix Australia)

options.text: Generic supportive counselling has equivalent efficacy to trauma focused therapy and is preferred in mild PTSD
options.rationale: Supportive counselling is less effective than trauma focused therapy for PTSD across severity levels. (Source: Phoenix Australia)

options.text: Trauma focused CBT and EMDR have comparable first-line evidence in adult PTSD and choice is guided by patient preference and clinician training
options.rationale: Both TF-CBT and EMDR are first-line for adult PTSD in AU; choice is guided by availability, clinician expertise, and patient preference. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD)

options.text: Sertraline is the recommended first-line treatment for adult PTSD before any trauma focused psychological therapy is offered
options.rationale: Trauma focused psychological therapies are first-line; SSRIs are an alternative or adjunct, not the recommended first step. (Source: Phoenix Australia)

options.text: Critical incident debriefing within a single session is the recommended first step in management of all adult PTSD
options.rationale: Single-session debriefing is not effective and may be harmful; structured trauma focused therapy is recommended instead. (Source: Phoenix Australia)

explanation.summary: Adult PTSD is treated first-line with a trauma focused psychological therapy. The Phoenix Australia guidelines recommend TF-CBT and EMDR as comparable first-line options. SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine, venlafaxine) are an alternative or adjunct, particularly for patients who decline psychotherapy or have prominent comorbid depression. Single-session debriefing is not recommended. Co-occurring substance use, depression, or chronic pain are addressed concurrently with PTSD treatment rather than sequentially.
key_points: TF-CBT and EMDR equally first-line for adult PTSD
SSRIs are alternative or adjunct treatment
Single-session debriefing not recommended
Treat comorbid depression or substance use concurrently
explanation.pearls: Prazosin 1 to 4 mg nocte can reduce trauma related nightmares in PTSD where sleep disturbance dominates; consider adding when nightmares persist despite psychotherapy.

subtopic_detail: Antibiotic choice for moderate COPD exacerbation in ED
stem: You are the medical registrar in the emergency department with a 68-year-old man presenting with 48 hours of worsening dyspnoea, increased sputum volume, and sputum purulence on a background of GOLD stage 3 COPD. He has had 2 exacerbations in the last year. He takes a long acting bronchodilator and inhaled corticosteroid daily. He has no penicillin allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.2, HR 102, RR 24, SpO2 88 percent on air rising to 92 percent on 2 L/min nasal prongs, BP 138/82
data_table.Examination: Bilateral wheeze, accessory muscle use, no focal consolidation
data_table.Investigations: ABG on 2 L pH 7.36, PaCO2 50, PaO2 70, HCO3 28; CXR no consolidation; CRP 38; FBC normal
data_table.Medications: Tiotropium plus formoterol budesonide combined inhaler daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics and treat with prednisolone 50 mg daily for 5 days alone given absence of fever
options.rationale: Three Anthonisen criteria (dyspnoea, sputum volume, sputum purulence) support antibiotic use even in the absence of fever. (Source: Lung Foundation COPD-X)

options.text: Oral azithromycin 500 mg daily for 5 days alongside oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Azithromycin is reserved for penicillin-allergic patients due to AU macrolide resistance patterns in Streptococcus pneumoniae. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin clavulanate 875 plus 125 mg twice daily for 5 days alongside oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Clavulanate is reserved for failed first-line therapy or risk factors for resistance; routine first-line is amoxicillin alone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Oral moxifloxacin 400 mg daily for 5 days alongside oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Moxifloxacin is reserved for failed first-line therapy or specific resistance; routine use is discouraged due to QT and tendinopathy risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 5 days alongside oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: AU first-line for moderate exacerbation is amoxicillin for 5 days plus prednisolone 5 days; the combination reduces treatment failure and hospital readmission. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: AU practice treats COPD exacerbation with three Anthonisen criteria with amoxicillin first-line for 5 days plus prednisolone 50 mg daily for 5 days. Doxycycline is an alternative when penicillin is not tolerated. Amoxicillin clavulanate or fluoroquinolones are reserved for failed first-line therapy, severe exacerbation requiring hospitalisation, or risk factors for resistance. Oxygen target in established COPD is SpO2 88 to 92 percent to avoid worsening hypercapnia.
key_points: Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS 5 days plus prednisolone 50 mg 5 days
Doxycycline alternative for penicillin allergy
SpO2 target 88 to 92 percent in COPD
Reserve clavulanate or fluoroquinolone for failure
explanation.pearls: Eosinophil count over 0.3 x10^9/L during exacerbation predicts steroid responsiveness and a lower threshold to use systemic corticosteroids.

subtopic_detail: Occupational HIV postexposure prophylaxis timing and regimen
stem: You are the staff health registrar reviewing a 28-year-old nurse 2 hours after a deep needlestick injury from a hollow bore needle used on a patient who is HIV positive on antiretroviral therapy with a viral load over 50000 copies/mL. The nurse has no chronic illness and is currently well. The source patient is also hepatitis B and C negative.

data_table.Examination: Wound puncture to right index finger; no active bleeding now
data_table.Investigations: Baseline HIV antibody pending; baseline UEC, LFTs, FBC, beta-hCG normal; hepatitis B antibody titre 120 IU/L; source HIV viral load over 50000, hepatitis B and C negative
data_table.Medications: Combined oral contraceptive; baseline HIV testing in progress

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Start a 28 day course of tenofovir disoproxil emtricitabine plus dolutegravir as recommended PEP within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: AU PEP guidance recommends a 3 drug regimen of TDF/FTC plus dolutegravir for 28 days starting within 72 hours of high-risk exposure. (Source: ASHM PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Defer PEP until source patient viral load is confirmed below 200 copies/mL by repeat testing within 24 hours
options.rationale: PEP is initiated immediately for high-risk exposure and not delayed for further source testing when initial viral load is detectable. (Source: ASHM)

options.text: Start a 28 day course of zidovudine plus lamivudine as the recommended dual NRTI backbone in AU guidance
options.rationale: Zidovudine has been superseded by tenofovir-based regimens in current AU PEP guidance due to tolerability and efficacy. (Source: ASHM)

options.text: Provide written advice on signs of seroconversion without antiretroviral prophylaxis given the use of source antiretroviral therapy
options.rationale: Use of antiretroviral therapy in a source with detectable high viral load does not eliminate transmission risk; PEP remains indicated. (Source: ASHM)

options.text: Test source for HIV drug resistance before commencing any antiretroviral postexposure prophylaxis regimen
options.rationale: Resistance testing should not delay PEP initiation; modification can be made if resistance is later identified. (Source: ASHM)

explanation.summary: Occupational HIV postexposure prophylaxis is recommended for high-risk exposures including deep injury from a hollow bore needle in a source with detectable HIV. The AU first-line regimen is tenofovir disoproxil plus emtricitabine plus dolutegravir for 28 days. PEP should commence within 72 hours and ideally within 2 hours of exposure. Baseline HIV, hepatitis B and C, and pregnancy testing are arranged with repeat HIV testing at 6 weeks and 3 months. Source resistance can guide regimen changes during the 28 day course.
key_points: Start PEP within 72 hours of high-risk exposure
First-line: tenofovir DF plus emtricitabine plus dolutegravir for 28 days
Follow-up HIV testing at 6 weeks and 3 months
Hepatitis B booster if antibody under 10 IU/L
explanation.pearls: Bictegravir based fixed dose regimens are increasingly used in AU PEP due to single tablet dosing and excellent tolerability; dolutegravir plus tenofovir emtricitabine remains widely available first-line.

subtopic_detail: BP reduction target and agent in hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage
stem: You are the medical registrar in ED reviewing a 58-year-old man with severe headache, blurred vision, and acute breathlessness over 6 hours. His BP is 232/132 with no prior diagnosis of hypertension. He has bibasal crackles, a third heart sound, troponin elevation, and pulmonary oedema on chest X-ray.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 232/132, HR 118, RR 30, SpO2 92 percent on 4 L/min
data_table.Examination: Bibasal crackles, S3, raised JVP, no focal neurology, fundi show flame haemorrhages and papilloedema
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia, LV strain pattern; CXR pulmonary oedema; troponin 0.18 (upper limit 0.04); creatinine 142; urinalysis 2+ protein; CT brain unremarkable
data_table.Medications: Nil regular; today: IV furosemide 80 mg and nitrate sublingual given

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial BP reduction strategy?

options.text: Withhold further antihypertensive therapy until troponin trend confirms or excludes acute myocardial infarction
options.rationale: Hypertensive pulmonary oedema with troponin rise mandates immediate BP reduction; waiting for troponin trend delays critical therapy. (Source: NHF)

options.text: Reduce systolic BP to under 140 within the first hour with rapid bolus dosing of intravenous antihypertensive agents
options.rationale: Rapid normalisation in hypertensive emergency risks watershed cerebral infarction and acute kidney injury; controlled stepwise reduction is recommended. (Source: NHF)

options.text: Reduce systolic BP to under 100 over the next 4 hours using sodium nitroprusside at high titration step infusion
options.rationale: Over-reduction to systolic under 100 risks watershed ischaemia; goal is staged reduction not normalisation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Start oral amlodipine 10 mg now and reassess at 1 hour expecting a stepwise reduction over the next 6 hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy is too slow and unpredictable in true hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage; IV titratable agents are required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Reduce mean arterial pressure by 20 to 25 percent over the first hour using an intravenous agent then aim 160/100 over the next several hours
options.rationale: Hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage other than ischaemic stroke is treated with controlled MAP reduction of 20 to 25 percent in the first hour to balance perfusion and tissue protection. (Source: NHF Hypertension; Therapeutic Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Hypertensive emergency is defined by severe BP elevation with acute end-organ damage including encephalopathy, pulmonary oedema, acute coronary syndrome, AKI, or papilloedema. Controlled BP reduction targets a 20 to 25 percent fall in MAP in the first hour, then 160/100 over several hours, with normalisation over 24 to 48 hours. Agents used in pulmonary oedema include IV glyceryl trinitrate; in aortic dissection, esmolol then sodium nitroprusside or labetalol; in eclampsia, hydralazine or labetalol. Ischaemic stroke has its own permissive hypertension targets.
key_points: MAP reduction 20-25 percent in first hour
Normalisation over 24-48 hours not minutes
GTN for pulmonary oedema; esmolol then SNP for dissection
Aim 160/100 over the next several hours
explanation.pearls: In acute ischaemic stroke, do not lower BP unless above 220/120 (or above 185/110 if thrombolysis planned), as cerebral autoregulation is impaired and over-reduction worsens infarction.

subtopic_detail: Hour-1 bundle interventions in septic shock from urinary source
stem: You are the medical registrar called to the ward at 23 minutes after midnight to assess a 74-year-old woman day 1 admission for pyelonephritis. She has rigors, mottling, and altered mental state. Initial empirical antibiotics for pyelonephritis were given 3 hours ago but her condition has deteriorated.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2, HR 132, RR 32, SpO2 94 percent on 4 L/min, BP 78/42
data_table.Examination: Mottled peripheries, cool, confused (GCS 13), right flank tenderness
data_table.Investigations: VBG lactate 4.8 pH 7.28 BE -8; WCC 21, neutrophils 18; CRP 280; creatinine 162 (baseline 78); urinalysis 4+ leucocytes, 3+ nitrites; blood cultures taken on admission and now
data_table.Medications: IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily started 3 hours ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next set of immediate interventions in the first hour from recognition of septic shock?

options.text: Crystalloid 10 mL/kg bolus and reassess; defer antibiotic broadening until repeat blood culture results are available tomorrow
options.rationale: 10 mL/kg is below the 30 mL/kg hour-1 target and antibiotic escalation should be considered immediately with rapid clinical deterioration. (Source: SSC)

options.text: Crystalloid 30 mL/kg bolus over the first hour, repeat blood cultures and lactate, broaden antibiotics, start vasopressor for MAP under 65
options.rationale: The hour-1 bundle for septic shock includes 30 mL/kg crystalloid, lactate measurement, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and vasopressors for refractory hypotension. (Source: SSC Guidelines; CEC NSW Sepsis)

options.text: Transfer to ICU for invasive line placement before any fluid or antibiotic action is taken now on the ward
options.rationale: Hour-1 interventions begin where the patient is recognised; ICU transfer must not delay fluid, antibiotics, or vasopressors. (Source: CEC NSW Sepsis)

options.text: Administer hydrocortisone 200 mg IV before fluid bolus as first step in shock from any source in older adults
options.rationale: Steroids in septic shock are reserved for refractory shock on adequate vasopressor support not as first-line therapy. (Source: SSC)

options.text: Start noradrenaline infusion immediately as first step before any fluid resuscitation in the setting of MAP under 65
options.rationale: Concurrent fluid resuscitation should occur with vasopressor commencement; vasopressor alone without fluid does not address hypovolaemia. (Source: SSC)

explanation.summary: The hour-1 bundle for sepsis and septic shock comprises measure lactate and remeasure if elevated, blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate over 4, and noradrenaline for MAP under 65 mmHg despite fluid resuscitation. Source control is sought as soon as feasible. Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV is added for refractory septic shock requiring escalating vasopressor support.
key_points: 30 mL/kg crystalloid in first hour for hypotension or lactate over 4
Blood cultures before antibiotics where possible
Noradrenaline first-line vasopressor for MAP under 65
Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV daily for refractory septic shock
explanation.pearls: Beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside is appropriate empirical cover in adult Australian community pyelonephritis with septic shock when local E. coli ESBL rates are above 10 percent.

subtopic_detail: Identifying the underlying mechanism of metabolic alkalosis with hypokalaemia
stem: You are the general medical registrar reviewing a 56-year-old woman with resistant hypertension despite three agents who has been intermittently using furosemide tablets sourced from her sister. She presents with weakness and muscle cramps over 1 week. She is normovolaemic on examination. She denies vomiting, laxative use, or recent diarrhoea.

data_table.Examination: Normal volume status, no oedema, no abdominal mass
data_table.Investigations: Na 138, K 2.6, Cl 88, HCO3 36, urea 6.8, creatinine 78; ABG pH 7.52 PaCO2 49 HCO3 36; urine chloride 32 mmol/L; urine sodium 64; renin and aldosterone pending
data_table.Medications: Perindopril, amlodipine, hydrochlorothiazide, and intermittent furosemide

lead_in: What is the most likely mechanism contributing to her metabolic alkalosis with hypokalaemia?

options.text: Primary hyperaldosteronism with autonomous adrenal aldosterone production driving the entire biochemical picture
options.rationale: Primary hyperaldosteronism is possible and is being investigated but the urinary chloride rise during current diuretic use is most directly explained by diuretic effect. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Diuretic induced chloride and potassium loss producing a saline responsive chloride low metabolic alkalosis
options.rationale: Loop and thiazide diuretics produce urinary chloride and potassium loss with secondary metabolic alkalosis; urine chloride may be elevated during active use. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Surreptitious vomiting with gastric acid loss producing chloride low metabolic alkalosis with low urine chloride
options.rationale: Vomiting produces low urine chloride below 20 mmol/L; this patient has urine chloride of 32 mmol/L during diuretic use. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Bartter syndrome with congenital tubular dysfunction producing chronic chloride and potassium losses
options.rationale: Bartter syndrome usually presents earlier in life and is associated with normal blood pressure; this presentation is acquired and hypertensive. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Acute respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation accounting for the elevated bicarbonate and PaCO2
options.rationale: The pH of 7.52 indicates alkalosis not acidosis; the elevated PaCO2 is respiratory compensation for metabolic alkalosis. (Source: KHA-CARI)

explanation.summary: Metabolic alkalosis with hypokalaemia is classified by urine chloride into saline responsive (low urine chloride from vomiting or post-diuretic state) and saline unresponsive (high urine chloride from current diuretic use, hyperaldosteronism, or Bartter and Gitelman syndromes). Surreptitious diuretic use produces a confusing biochemical picture mimicking primary hyperaldosteronism; the clue is variable urine chloride that falls when diuretics are stopped. Definitive workup includes urine diuretic screen, plasma renin, and aldosterone with aldosterone renin ratio.
key_points: Low urine chloride: vomiting or post-diuretic effect
High urine chloride: current diuretic, hyperaldosteronism, Bartter/Gitelman
Hyperaldosteronism workup with ARR after diuretic cessation
Replace potassium and magnesium together
explanation.pearls: Urine diuretic screen for thiazide and loop diuretics is a reliable test for surreptitious diuretic use and should be considered when biochemistry mimics primary hyperaldosteronism.

subtopic_detail: Induction therapy choice for newly diagnosed ANCA-associated vasculitis with renal involvement
stem: You are the general medical registrar admitting a 62-year-old man with 6 weeks of weight loss, sinusitis with crusting, haemoptysis, and progressive renal impairment over 3 weeks. He has no infection and is haemodynamically stable. ANCA testing is strongly positive for PR3. Renal biopsy shows pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis. He has a stable haemoglobin and no requirement for dialysis at present.

data_table.Examination: Nasal crusting; bilateral basal crackles; lower limb purpura sparse
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 320 (baseline 90), eGFR 18, urine red cell casts; PR3-ANCA strongly positive; chest CT pulmonary nodules; renal biopsy crescentic GN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate induction regimen?

options.text: Azathioprine plus oral prednisolone as the initial induction regimen with cyclophosphamide reserved for relapse only
options.rationale: Azathioprine is a maintenance agent; it is not effective for induction in AAV which requires rituximab or cyclophosphamide. (Source: KDIGO)

options.text: Plasma exchange alone as primary induction therapy when renal involvement and pulmonary haemorrhage are present
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is an adjunct in selected severe disease and does not replace immunosuppressive induction. (Source: KDIGO)

options.text: Pulse methylprednisolone monotherapy at 1 g IV daily for 3 days as adequate induction therapy in renal involvement
options.rationale: Steroid monotherapy is inadequate induction; B cell depletion or cyclophosphamide is required to reduce flare and end stage kidney disease. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Rituximab plus high dose glucocorticoid as induction therapy with rapid steroid tapering protocol over the next several months
options.rationale: Rituximab plus high dose glucocorticoid is at least equivalent to cyclophosphamide for induction in ANCA-associated vasculitis including renal disease and is increasingly preferred in AU. (Source: KHA-CARI; KDIGO Glomerular Disease)

options.text: Mycophenolate mofetil plus oral prednisolone as the recommended first-line induction therapy in PR3 positive AAV
options.rationale: Mycophenolate is less effective than rituximab or cyclophosphamide for AAV induction and is not first-line in PR3 disease. (Source: KDIGO)

explanation.summary: ANCA-associated vasculitis with renal involvement is induced with rituximab plus high dose glucocorticoid or cyclophosphamide plus glucocorticoid. Rituximab is at least as effective as cyclophosphamide and is preferred for relapsing disease, women of reproductive age, and patients at high infection risk. Plasma exchange is reserved for severe pulmonary haemorrhage or rapidly progressive AKI. Maintenance is typically rituximab or azathioprine with a steroid taper; PJP prophylaxis with co-trimoxazole is recommended during prolonged immunosuppression.
key_points: Rituximab plus glucocorticoid first-line induction
Cyclophosphamide plus glucocorticoid alternative induction
Plasma exchange for severe pulmonary haemorrhage
PJP prophylaxis during prolonged immunosuppression
explanation.pearls: PEXIVAS trial supports a reduced dose glucocorticoid regimen with similar efficacy and lower serious infection risk; AU practice is moving away from the historical high-dose taper.

subtopic_detail: Correction rate in chronic hyponatraemia with mild neurological symptoms
stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 72-year-old woman with 2 weeks of progressive nausea, gait unsteadiness, and a fall today. She has been on a thiazide for 6 months and recently started taking sertraline 2 weeks ago. She has no seizure, no severe headache, and remains alert. She is clinically euvolaemic and well hydrated.

data_table.Examination: Alert, ataxic gait, no focal neurology, euvolaemic mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: Na 116, K 3.6, urea 4.2, creatinine 78, glucose 5.4, TSH normal, cortisol AM 320; urine osmolality 480, urine sodium 64, plasma osmolality 248
data_table.Medications: Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily; sertraline 50 mg daily (started 2 weeks ago); calcium and vitamin D

lead_in: What is the most appropriate correction strategy over the next 24 hours?

options.text: Aim to raise serum sodium by 4 to 6 mmol/L in the first 6 hours then no more than 10 mmol/L over 24 hours with frequent reassessment
options.rationale: Chronic hyponatraemia with mild neurology is corrected at a controlled rate of 4 to 6 mmol/L in the first 6 hours and under 10 mmol/L per day to prevent osmotic demyelination. (Source: KHA-CARI; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Restrict fluids to 800 mL daily without changing existing medications and reassess sodium tomorrow morning
options.rationale: Fluid restriction alone is insufficient in a symptomatic patient and the offending medications should be ceased. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Start hypertonic saline infusion targeting a serum sodium rise of 1 to 2 mmol/L per hour throughout the next 12 hours
options.rationale: Continuous 1 to 2 mmol/L per hour rise overshoots the safe 24 hour limit and risks osmotic demyelination. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Aim to normalise serum sodium to over 135 mmol/L within the first 24 hours using 3 percent hypertonic saline boluses
options.rationale: Rapid full correction risks osmotic demyelination; cautious rate-limited correction is the standard. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Withhold all fluid replacement and observe with hourly neurological assessment for the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Active correction is required in symptomatic hyponatraemia; observation alone does not address the cerebral oedema risk. (Source: KHA-CARI)

explanation.summary: Chronic hyponatraemia is corrected slowly at 4 to 6 mmol/L in the first 6 hours and under 10 mmol/L over 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome. Severely symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizures or coma is treated with bolus 3 percent saline 150 mL until symptoms resolve. Cessation of offending agents (thiazide, SSRI) and management of underlying SIADH with fluid restriction follow. Serum sodium is checked every 2 to 4 hours during active correction.
key_points: Chronic: under 10 mmol/L per 24 hours, 4 to 6 in first 6 hours
Severely symptomatic: 150 mL 3 percent saline boluses
Cease thiazide and SSRI; rule out adrenal and thyroid causes
Overcorrection risks osmotic demyelination
explanation.pearls: If sodium has risen over 8 mmol/L in 24 hours during correction, dextrose 5 percent at 6 mL/kg over 2 hours or DDAVP 2 to 4 micrograms IV can re-lower sodium to prevent osmotic demyelination.

stem: You are the endocrinology registrar planning preoperative care for a 48-year-old woman with confirmed adrenal phaeochromocytoma on biochemistry (24 hour fractionated metanephrines 5 times upper limit) and a 5 cm right adrenal mass on CT. She has episodic headache, palpitations, and sweating. Her resting BP is 158/92 with paroxysms to 220/120. She has had no cardiac events and her echocardiogram shows normal LV function.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 158/92 with paroxysms to 220/120; HR 90 baseline, 130 during paroxysm
data_table.Examination: No tremor at rest; no thyroid enlargement; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: 24 hour fractionated metanephrines 5 times upper limit; CT adrenal 5 cm right mass; ECG sinus rhythm; echocardiogram normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step in preoperative preparation?

options.text: Commence phenoxybenzamine and titrate over 2 to 3 weeks before introducing a beta blocker if tachycardia persists
options.rationale: Alpha blockade is established before any beta blocker introduction in phaeochromocytoma to prevent unopposed alpha-mediated hypertensive crisis. (Source: AU Endocrine Society; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Commence amlodipine 5 mg daily as a calcium channel blocker first-line before considering more specific therapy
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockers are second-line adjunct after established alpha blockade; not first-line preoperative preparation. (Source: AU Endocrine Society)

options.text: Proceed to surgical resection within the next week without further pharmacological preoperative preparation today
options.rationale: Adequate alpha blockade for at least 10 to 14 days reduces intraoperative haemodynamic instability and morbidity. (Source: AU Endocrine Society)

options.text: Commence labetalol 100 mg twice daily as a combined alpha and beta blocker for outpatient preoperative preparation
options.rationale: Labetalol provides predominantly beta blockade and is not recommended as first-line for preoperative phaeochromocytoma preparation. (Source: AU Endocrine Society)

options.text: Commence atenolol 50 mg daily first to control the resting tachycardia and add alpha blocker if BP control is incomplete
options.rationale: Unopposed alpha stimulation after beta blockade can precipitate hypertensive crisis in phaeochromocytoma. (Source: AU Endocrine Society)

explanation.summary: Preoperative preparation for phaeochromocytoma uses alpha blockade with phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin for at least 10 to 14 days before surgery. Beta blockade is introduced only after alpha blockade is established if reflex tachycardia or arrhythmia occurs. Liberal sodium intake and adequate hydration in the days before surgery prevent post-resection hypotension. Calcium channel blockers can be used as adjuncts. Genetic counselling is considered for SDHB and other heritable syndromes especially in younger patients or extra-adrenal disease.
key_points: Alpha blockade first then beta if needed
10 to 14 days minimum alpha blockade preop
Liberal salt and hydration to prevent post-op hypotension
Genetic counselling for younger or extra-adrenal disease
explanation.pearls: Persistent post-resection hypotension may require IV crystalloid plus noradrenaline due to abrupt loss of catecholamine drive; preoperative volume expansion reduces this risk.

subtopic_detail: Differentiating ectopic ACTH from pituitary Cushing disease using dynamic and imaging tests
stem: You are the endocrinology registrar reviewing a 51-year-old man with 8 weeks of rapidly progressive proximal weakness, central weight gain, refractory hypokalaemia, glucose intolerance, and now bilateral lower limb oedema. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history. His skin is hyperpigmented but he has no thin skin or purple striae. Initial screening confirms ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome. He has commenced ketoconazole while workup proceeds.

data_table.Examination: Central adiposity; hyperpigmentation of palmar creases; bilateral oedema; no abdominal striae
data_table.Investigations: 24 hour urine cortisol 5 times upper limit; midnight salivary cortisol elevated; ACTH 280 (normal under 50); 8 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression: cortisol 980 (under 50 percent suppression); pituitary MRI no adenoma; CT chest 18 mm left lower lobe nodule; potassium 2.6 on supplementation
data_table.Medications: Ketoconazole 200 mg twice daily; potassium chloride 600 mg twice daily; perindopril 10 mg daily

lead_in: Which feature most strongly favours ectopic ACTH syndrome rather than pituitary Cushing disease as the cause?

options.text: Absence of a visible pituitary lesion on MRI of the sella reliably rules out pituitary Cushing disease entirely
options.rationale: Up to 40 percent of pituitary Cushing adenomas are below MRI resolution; absent MRI lesion does not exclude pituitary disease. (Source: AU Endocrine Society)

options.text: Hyperpigmentation of palmar creases without other findings is itself diagnostic of ectopic ACTH syndrome in adults
options.rationale: Hyperpigmentation is seen in any ACTH-dependent state including severe pituitary Cushing disease and is not specifically discriminating. (Source: AU Endocrine Society)

options.text: A 24 hour urine cortisol level of more than 4 times the upper limit reliably distinguishes ectopic from pituitary disease
options.rationale: Magnitude of urine cortisol elevation overlaps between aetiologies and is not a discriminating test. (Source: AU Endocrine Society)

options.text: Failure of cortisol to suppress below 50 percent after high dose dexamethasone in combination with marked hypokalaemia and rapid onset
options.rationale: Failure to suppress on 8 mg dexamethasone, severe hypokalaemia from cortisol-mediated mineralocorticoid effect, and rapid onset are typical of ectopic ACTH rather than pituitary disease. (Source: AU Endocrine Society Cushing; KDIGO)

options.text: ACTH level over 100 picograms/mL is itself characteristic of pituitary Cushing rather than ectopic ACTH syndrome
options.rationale: Higher ACTH levels typically favour ectopic ACTH; pituitary Cushing more often produces moderate ACTH elevation. (Source: AU Endocrine Society)

explanation.summary: Discrimination of ectopic ACTH from pituitary Cushing disease relies on the high dose dexamethasone suppression test, CRH stimulation, ACTH magnitude, and inferior petrosal sinus sampling when imaging is inconclusive. Ectopic ACTH typically does not suppress on high dose dexamethasone, presents with marked hypokalaemia from cortisol mineralocorticoid effect, and progresses rapidly. Pituitary Cushing usually suppresses by over 50 percent on high dose dexamethasone, has a more indolent course, and is amenable to transsphenoidal surgery. Small cell lung cancer and bronchial carcinoid are common ectopic sources. Inferior petrosal sinus sampling is the gold standard discriminator when biochemistry and imaging conflict.
key_points: Ectopic ACTH: no suppression on 8 mg dexamethasone, severe hypokalaemia, rapid onset
Pituitary Cushing: over 50 percent suppression on 8 mg dexamethasone
MRI misses up to 40 percent of pituitary microadenomas
IPSS is the gold standard when discrimination is unclear
explanation.pearls: Ketoconazole, metyrapone, and osilodrostat are used to control cortisol while definitive treatment is planned; etomidate infusion is the rapid parenteral option for severe Cushing crisis.
